title: Intro to Literary Devices
summary: Learn the core purpose of literary devices and recognize the first ten devices in context.
body_markdown:
# Intro to Literary Devices

Writers use literary devices to make meaning stick. A device can sharpen an image, create a sound pattern, hint at what is coming, or make a simple sentence carry a bigger idea.

## How to read for devices

Start with what the words are doing. Are they comparing, exaggerating, hinting, making a sound, or pointing to a larger idea? Then name the device and explain the effect.

## Devices in this path

Metaphor, simile, personification, imagery, alliteration, hyperbole, irony, symbolism, foreshadowing, and onomatopoeia.

title: Applying Devices in Context
summary: Practice choosing devices that match a writer’s purpose and improve plain sentences.
body_markdown:
# Applying Devices in Context

Recognizing a device is only the first step. Strong readers also explain why a writer chose it.

## Match device to purpose

Use metaphor or simile to build comparison. Use imagery to make a moment sensory. Use symbolism when an object needs to carry an idea. Use foreshadowing when a detail should prepare readers for what comes next.

## A useful sentence frame

_The writer uses [device] in “[words]” to [effect]._ This keeps your answer tied to the text.

title: Combining Evidence and Effect
summary: Learn how to compare similar devices and explain their effect in a short analysis.
body_markdown:
# Combining Evidence and Effect

Some devices overlap. Metaphor and simile both compare. Alliteration and onomatopoeia both involve sound. Symbolism and foreshadowing can both make a detail feel important.

## Explain the difference

Look for the signal. Like or as usually points to simile. Sound imitation points to onomatopoeia. A future hint points to foreshadowing. A concrete object carrying a larger idea points to symbolism.

## Build a complete response

Name the device, quote the key words, and explain how those words shape meaning or mood.
